---
title: "Understanding Similarity Scores"
subtitle: "Sorensen-Dice, Tanimoto, and cosine similarity explained"
---

## Goal

Every search on the platform produces a similarity score for each hit. This page explains what each score means in plain language, how to interpret its value, and what to watch out for when comparing scores across different search methods.

## When to use this page

Refer to this page whenever you are unsure what a similarity score in your search results means, or when you want to set an appropriate threshold for a new query.

## The three similarity metrics

The platform uses three different similarity metrics, one per search method. All three produce scores on a 0-to-1 scale, where higher values indicate greater similarity --- but the numbers are **not directly comparable** across methods.

### Summary table

| Search method | Metric | What is compared | Score range | Typical "good match" |
|---------------|--------|-------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| [Domain Query](domain-query.qmd) | Sorensen-Dice coefficient | Pfam domain sets | 0--1 | 0.7+ |
| [Sequence Search](sequence-search.qmd) | Cosine similarity | Protein embeddings (1,152-dimensional vectors) | 0--1 | 0.7+ |
| [Chemical Search](chemical-search.qmd) | Tanimoto coefficient | Morgan fingerprints of product structures | 0--1 | 0.85+ |

## Sorensen-Dice coefficient (domain query)

### What it measures

The Sorensen-Dice coefficient quantifies the overlap between two sets. In the context of domain queries, it compares the set of Pfam domains you specified in your query (*A*) with the set of Pfam domains annotated in each BGC (*B*).

### Formula

$$
\text{Dice}(A, B) = \frac{2 \times |A \cap B|}{|A| + |B|}
$$

- $|A \cap B|$ is the number of domains present in both sets.
- $|A|$ and $|B|$ are the sizes of each set.

### Plain-language interpretation

The Dice coefficient answers the question: *"Of all the domains mentioned by either my query or the BGC, what fraction is shared?"* The factor of 2 in the numerator means that shared domains are double-counted, which makes Dice slightly more generous than the Jaccard index for partial overlaps.

### Score guide

| Score | Interpretation |
|-------|---------------|
| 1.0 | The BGC contains exactly the domains you searched for, and no others. In practice this is rare for large BGCs. |
| 0.8--0.99 | Very strong match. The BGC contains most or all of your query domains plus a few additional ones (or vice versa). |
| 0.6--0.8 | Moderate match. There is meaningful overlap but several domains differ. |
| 0.4--0.6 | Weak match. Some shared domains but substantial differences. |
| < 0.4 | Little overlap. The BGC is unlikely to be closely related to your query. |

### Things to keep in mind

- A small query set (e.g. one domain) can produce high Dice scores for BGCs that contain many domains, because the single shared domain still contributes heavily to the numerator.
- Conversely, a very large query set penalises BGCs that contain only a subset of the requested domains. Consider using OR logic if you are not sure all domains will be present.
- Dice does not account for domain copy number --- a BGC with three ketosynthase copies is treated the same as one with a single copy.

## Cosine similarity (sequence search)

### What it measures

Cosine similarity compares two protein embeddings --- high-dimensional numerical vectors generated by a protein language model (ESM). Each embedding captures the biochemical properties, evolutionary relationships, and structural features of a protein in 1,152 numbers.

### Formula

$$
\text{cosine}(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{|\vec{u}| \times |\vec{v}|}
$$

- $\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}$ is the dot product of the two vectors.
- $|\vec{u}|$ and $|\vec{v}|$ are their magnitudes (lengths).

### Plain-language interpretation

Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, ignoring their length. If two protein embeddings point in exactly the same direction, the cosine is 1.0 --- the proteins are effectively identical in the model's learned representation. As the angle between them grows, the cosine drops toward 0.

### Score guide

| Score | Interpretation |
|-------|---------------|
| 0.95--1.0 | Near-identical proteins. Likely the same protein or very close orthologs. |
| 0.80--0.95 | Strong similarity. Proteins probably share the same function and fold. |
| 0.65--0.80 | Moderate similarity. Related protein family; may have divergent functions. |
| 0.50--0.65 | Weak similarity. Distant relationship; worth investigating but not guaranteed to be functionally relevant. |
| < 0.50 | Unlikely to be related. The platform uses 0.5 as the lower bound for search results. |

### Things to keep in mind

- Cosine similarity in embedding space does **not** correspond directly to percent sequence identity. Two proteins at 40% identity might have a cosine similarity of 0.85 if they share the same fold and function; two proteins at 60% identity might score lower if they have diverged functionally.
- The embedding captures global protein properties. Local motifs (e.g. a single active-site residue change) may not dramatically alter the embedding.
- The search compares your query protein against the **representative embedding** of each BGC, which summarises all proteins in the cluster. This means a BGC will score high if it contains at least one protein very similar to your query, even if the other proteins in the cluster are unrelated.

## Tanimoto coefficient (chemical search)

### What it measures

The Tanimoto coefficient compares Morgan fingerprints --- binary vectors that encode the substructural features present in each molecule. It quantifies how much of each molecule's structural inventory is shared with the other.

### Formula

$$
T_c(A, B) = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}
$$

- $|A \cap B|$ is the number of fingerprint bits set to 1 in both molecules.
- $|A \cup B|$ is the number of bits set to 1 in at least one molecule.

### Plain-language interpretation

The Tanimoto coefficient answers: *"Of all the structural features found in either molecule, what fraction is shared by both?"* Unlike Dice, there is no double-counting of shared features, making Tanimoto slightly more conservative.

### Score guide

| Score | Interpretation |
|-------|---------------|
| 0.95--1.0 | Near-identical structures. Likely the same compound or a very minor analog (e.g. a single methylation). |
| 0.85--0.95 | Close analogs. Same core scaffold with different substituents. |
| 0.70--0.85 | Related scaffolds. Recognisably similar chemical family but meaningful structural differences. |
| 0.50--0.70 | Loosely related. May share a ring system or functional group but overall different structures. |
| < 0.50 | Unrelated structures. |

### Things to keep in mind

- Tanimoto is sensitive to molecule size. A small molecule compared against a much larger one will score lower than expected, because the larger molecule has many "extra" fingerprint bits that the small molecule lacks.
- Stereochemistry is only partially captured by Morgan fingerprints. Two stereoisomers may have identical or near-identical Tanimoto scores despite having different biological activities.
- The score reflects the **best-matching product** for each BGC. If a BGC has multiple predicted products, the highest Tanimoto score is reported.

## Combining multiple search methods

When you run more than one query type simultaneously (e.g. a domain query and a sequence search), the platform **intersects** the result sets. Only BGCs that meet the threshold for every active query appear in the final results.

For each BGC in the intersection, the results display the **minimum score** across the active methods. This tells you which criterion was hardest for that BGC to satisfy.

### Example

You run a domain query (Dice threshold 0.6) and a sequence search (cosine threshold 0.7) together. A BGC scores 0.82 on Dice and 0.75 on cosine. The displayed similarity is **0.75** (the lower of the two), flagging that the sequence match, while above threshold, was the weaker link.

## Practical guidelines for setting thresholds

1. **Start with defaults.** The platform sets reasonable defaults for each method. Run your first search at these settings.
2. **Look at the score distribution.** After a search, scan the Similarity column. If there is a clear gap (e.g. several hits at 0.90+ and then a drop to 0.60), that gap often marks a natural boundary between true positives and noise.
3. **Adjust incrementally.** Lower or raise the threshold by 0.05 at a time. This gives you fine control without dramatic jumps in result set size.
4. **Consider your goal.** If you are doing an initial survey, favour sensitivity (lower thresholds). If you are selecting BGCs for expensive downstream work (e.g. synthesis or cloning), favour specificity (higher thresholds).

## Related pages

- [Domain Query Builder](domain-query.qmd) --- uses Sorensen-Dice coefficient
- [Sequence Search](sequence-search.qmd) --- uses cosine similarity
- [Chemical Structure Search](chemical-search.qmd) --- uses Tanimoto coefficient
- [Reading Search Results](search-results.qmd) --- how scores appear in the results table
- [Search BGCs Overview](search-overview.qmd) --- the Search interface layout
